posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Jasper Morris does a deep dive on premox–informative and provocative, but also detailed on technical. (Perhaps not late night reading.)

World Of Fine Wine – 11 Nov 15

White Burgundy out of the Woods?

Exploring the imperfectly understood incidence in recent years of unduly advanced, sometimes completely oxidized bottles of wine - especially white Burgundy.

Est. reading time: 30 minutes
Thanks for posting, Joshua. Looks intriguing, and I agree, best read on a fresh mind.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Yes…thanks for posting.

I find it very frustrating that there is no source to learn about the latest “thinking” on this problem (as opposed to various people’s individual theories…which are…well…everyone has one stuff.)

Look forward to reading this, as it seems on first skim to fit the bill.

posts.name: Benjamin Hand
posts.cooked:
This is an excellent article, which I think sums up a great deal of the current thinking in a very non-technical manner. Lovers of white burgundy owe Jasper Morris a great deal of appreciation for writing about a controversial and fraught subject.

Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

I find it very frustrating that there is no source to learn about the latest “thinking” on this problem (as opposed to various people’s individual theories…which are…well…everyone has one stuff.)

What kind of source would offer more than “individual theories”? Do you mean something that attempted to aggregate new sources in one place?

Unfortunately, it seems to me that the most vigorous work mainly appears in French-language oenology journals. Denis Dubourdieu, as you likely already know, has published a series of articles on this exact subject, which shape how many winemakers perceive the problem. But they are officially available only in French I believe (although I could be mistaken).

posts.name: Peter Simpson
posts.cooked: I believe Jasper wrote this article in 2013 (see the 4th paragraph under “Is premox really oxidation?”), so maybe not quite the latest thinking on the subject.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
You are right, Peter, but it’s still the best compendium I’ve read. I wonder if there’s any new thinking in 2014-2015. Of course, Jasper’s article doesn’t really reach any conclusions…or provide anyone’s…or even offer an opinion. But, he does provide cogent bases for the “suspects”.

If anyone knows any updates’ substance, I’m very interested.

“Individual theories”, for me meant as opposed to an industry consensus. And, other than the corks’ accounting for the variability within a given case, I think we’re still left with a lack of any consensus…at least that I can deduce and rely on.

But, Jasper’s article , at least, organizes the various theories and information, if it mainly says that no one really knows the answers yet…or, more importantly, the solutions.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Yes - Stuart, I agree with what you said.

When there is a problem and there is a solution, then there is really no problem; as we just follow the solution to solve problem.

So when there is a problem and there is, so far, no solution - then I will have to accept there is a problem - in other words I have not been buying too, too many g-crus and 1er crus after vintage 2002. If I do buy them it will be maxi 3 bottles and drink them young…

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I think the problem is that there isn’t a problem to the people who have any control over the issue - i.e. the producers.

Their wines are selling well, fetching high and rising prices, what incentive do they have to do to search for the root causes, experiment with solutions, or do anything else differently than they are currently doing? What is the incentive? And especially what is the incentive for any single producer, most of whom make fairly small quantities, particularly of ageworthy bottlings, to try to tackle the issue?

Furthermore, the cause and the effect are spread so far in time, there is even less reason to try. If a producer today actually cared enough to consider doing some things differently, it wouldn’t be until 2024 or later when there would be any way to know if it worked.

So none of us should be expecting anything to change here. You either drink them in the first 6 years or so from the vintage, or you try to stick to producers with better track records and realize that you’re still taking a risk, and price that risk % into how you view the cost.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: Personally I didn’t see any new info here. That being said the most telling comment was concerning if people wanted wines to drink now expect nothing to change. I’m sure the great majority of their clientele want delicious wines now. Not in 10 years.

posts.name: Nigel Groundwater
posts.cooked:
Clearly for any improvements vis a vis premox ‘things’ had to change and there are many white Burgundy producers who recognised they had a problem and involved themselves with the authorities, universities and independent laboratories to research the wide range of issues that might obviously have been involved. Some were also able to do their own research and came to conclusions about what they needed to do.

For example Bouchard [and William Fevre within that Group] have certainly made major changes which inter alia included SO2 levels and practice, a reduced use of new oak, a gradual change from natural cork to DIAM from the bottom up i.e. years ago Fevre began with their basic Chablis, gradually included all their 1er cru wines and finally relinquished natural cork completely with their Grand Crus from 2008. Additionally they invested in a state-of-the-art bottling line with a key aim of controlling the amount of oxygen that could be introduced to/become entrained in the wine during the bottling process which was an problem that the extensive research into the premox phenomenon had identified as a potentially major issue.

These were changes that were extensively reported and which they were prepared to discuss when visited in order to purchase their wines in Chablis although there may well have been more steps taken e.g. related to pressing and other issues identified in Jasper’s article.

However what is identified above seems a clear indication that this producer took premox very seriously and has made major changes and investments to deal with the phenomenon. It will take time to judge the extent to which these steps have been successful.

Another major producer who had addressed closure issues [which included TCA of course] was Laroche who had move extensively to screwcap except where market demands required the continued use of cork. This occurred in 2001 before the premox phenomenon has become a mainstream issue and it is perhaps interesting that, according to recent report in Decanter, they have turned back to natural cork due to their embrace of a more reductive style of winemaking. AFAIK they have not completely abandoned the screwcap although one assumes that the improvement in the general quality of natural cork must have played some part in this change of practice bearing in mind Laroche’s earlier embrace and endorsement of the screwcap - and its reportedly very good performance.

What IMO seems clear is that closure choice really requires the winemaking to be taken into account and ironically it was perhaps the major changes in white Burgundy winemaking in the mid-90s without proper consideration of how it would work with the properties of the existing natural cork closure which might have played a significant role in what transpired just as the swift move to the screwcap Down Under brought its share of reductive SLO issues requiring copperfining and bluefining until due consideration was given to the winemaking and liner research and development provided another degree of freedom.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Somebody posted an article here by
Dubourdieu,et al…well worth checking out…

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
The most interesting things in that article to me were:

  1. Bottling machines (or the individual nozzles) can inject very different amounts of air into the bottle in the process of corking. This offers another possible explanation for the variability within a given case, beyond cork inconsistencies. It’s not an explanation in itself, I don’t believe – there must be some changes in the winemaking, as well, to make the wines more vulnerable. Still, interesting.

  2. The quality of corks (apart from TCA) declined in the 90s as worldwide wine consumption rose and very few producers had yet turned to screw caps or other alternative closures.

  3. Some aromas that appear to be premox aren’t necessarily that and clean up. I’ve experienced this many times with different wines and wondered how oxidation could disappear with air. Jasper Morris asks whether some white Burgundies are being misdiagnosed and dumped before they have enough air to clear up.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
John Morris:
  1. Some aromas that appear to be premox aren’t necessarily that and clean up. I’ve experienced this many times with different wines and wondered how oxidation could disappear with air. Jasper Morris asks whether some white Burgundies are being misdiagnosed and dumped before they have enough air to clear up.

Ummm…that is an interesting point, John. Many people on this board, perhaps even most, cannot successfully identify corked bottles with any consistency, for a whole host of reasons: little experience with corked wines (the more bottles one opens, the more experience one is likely to have); reduced sensitivity to TCA (everybody is different, apparently); the diagnosis being confounded by the bottle being only partially corked and perhaps older and exhibiting other characteristics or flaws that may push limited TCA taint to the background; and being in denial that a $15,000 bottle of DRC RC could possibly be corked. No doubt we have exactly the same problem in correctly diagnosing and understanding reduction in wine, and perhaps Jasper’s born-again 1996s suffered from reduction rather than premox, if time and air bring them back around. Indeed, Peter Wasserman, in an “I’ll Drink To That” interview, a guy who has forgotten more about Burgundy than most of us will ever know, flatly stated that the incidence of premox is much lower than we presently believe, and to the extent that it does exist, it will be found in other whites in California, Alsace, etc., and in some reds as well. (Jasper Morris concurs.) Peter thinks that reductive winemaking is often mistaken for premox, and given that reductive winemaking has been a growth industry for some time now, I am guessing that he is right. He says that he encounters many wines that are reductive and come around with time and air, but relatively few that are premoxed. I tend to trust his lifetime of drinking Burgundy to wine-board polls on premox. My own experiments with aeration, while focused mainly on Nebbiolo, strongly support the proposition that far too many bottles of wine (including some of mine) have been poured down the sink because their owners simply did not grasp the wine’s need for oxygen prior to drinking. Below is Meadows on the subject of reduction, from the November 1, 2011 issue of PinotFile (Volume 8, Issue 45; there is a rather unimpressive companion article by Kerith Overstreet, M.D., containing all manner of scientific mumbo-jumbo, as well). Food for thought…

"Allen Meadows on Reduction

Because of the continued use, in fact growth, of reductive winemaking in Burgundy, more and more in-barrel tastings that occur after malolactic fermentations, result in notes that refer to reduction. When wines are reduced, I say so rather than substituting a wine writing euphemism. p> The reason that the term reduction is seen so often is because more and more Burgundians, for both red and white wines, are now practicing what is called reductive winemaking. Effectively, what this means is that they are increasingly working with the fine lees; while this has long been accepted practice with whites, it has now spread to the reds as well. As a result, they rack less often or not at all and when they do, it’s often with limited or no exposure to oxygen. Indeed, more and more vignerons rack only in preparation for the bottling, which means that when I and other reviewers pass by to take a look at the wines a year after they were made, they are generally in a pretty reduced state.
Without getting into a chemical analysis (see Overstreet’s discussion below), of which I am ill-equipped to guide you anyway, this practice often results in a highly reductive environment in barrel, which simply put is the absence of oxygen. Winemakers have long understood that too much oxygen, particular in whites, is not a good thing because it oxidizes them and in extreme cases, can turn both reds and whites prematurely brown. However, the reverse can be taken to an extreme as well and when this occurs, a condition known as reduction occurs. A reduced wine smells dirty and in particular of sulfur compounds. A heavily reduced wine will taste of it as well and when extreme reduction occurs, mercaptans appear. Mercaptans smell of burnt rubber, garlic, stale sweat among other descriptors and suffice it to say, that is very unpleasant and if not corrected, can ruin a wine.
Reduction is, generally speaking, easily cured simply by introducing oxygen. As a practical matter, the way that this is most often done is to rack the wine from one barrel to another, which introduces oxygen and eliminates most of the lees (though sometimes they are kept and added back). However, if reduction appears in a finished, bottled wine, it is a clear flaw and while aeration (decanting) will usually clear it up, some wines are permanently reduced and about all that can be done is to put a penny in the wine. Sounds bizarre, but it works! (Note that my wife suggests a clean penny).

The reason that reduction is significant is because it renders a wine particularly difficult to judge. Slight reduction is not something to worry about because the basic characteristics of the wine are still evident. Heavy reduction that extends to the flavors makes a wine almost impossible to accurately judge and if the condition is left untreated it can permanently mark a wine. This risk explains why traditional practice in Burgundy is to rack a wine after the malolactic fermentation is finished so as to introduce some oxygen and thus dissipate the reductive aromas. However, with ever more precise analytical tools, a winemaker can push the edge of the envelope in this regard and still not reach a condition where more radical or invasive solutions are required to fix a problem. While this practice probably does result in richer and more complex wines, it makes the review process extremely difficult while the wines are in this state. I share this with you in the interest of full disclosure as I make every effort to judge a wine as accurately as possible but when there are limitations on my ability to do so, it’s incumbent upon me to say just that…It stands to reason that the closer any wine is to its finished state the more accurate the guidance will be."

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked:
I would be curious to see what the vacuum level recorded at the corker is for these wines. If they even measured.

Ideally a little positive pressure, or zero, is fine, but a very negative pressure can draw air into the wine through the cork postbottling.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Bill - A couple of responses…

I’m not talking about mistaking typical reductive aromas (sulfur, mercaptans) for oxidation, though some people may do that. Like Jasper Morris, I’m talking about aromas that smell like madeira – like classic oxidation. Sometimes those blow off and the wine emerges clean and fresh. So this is sort of faux oxidation.

As for premox in other wines, I think that’s widely acknowledged. (I experienced it with a 2003 Gulfi Carjcanti from Sicily in the mid-2000s. It was a lovely wine on release, but many bottles became oxidized quite soon and there was substantial bottle variation. This was a low sulfur wine, I believe.)

I’ve never considered California chardonnays that oxidized in five to seven years to be “pre-moxed.” Id’ call that normal, after being served many such wines over the last 20 years. Presumably that’s not for lack of sulfur. I’ve always assume it’s too little acid. Cal chardonnays that go longer are the exception.

posts.name: Bill Klapp (deactivated)
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

Bill - A couple of responses…

I’m not talking about mistaking typical reductive aromas (sulfur, mercaptans) for oxidation, though some people may do that. Like Jasper Morris, I’m talking about aromas that smell like madeira – like classic oxidation. Sometimes those blow off and the wine emerges clean and fresh. So this is sort of faux oxidation.

As for premox in other wines, I think that’s widely acknowledged. (I experienced it with a 2003 Gulfi Carjcanti from Sicily in the mid-2000s. It was a lovely wine on release, but many bottles became oxidized quite soon and there was substantial bottle variation. This was a low sulfur wine, I believe.)

I’ve never considered California chardonnays that oxidized in five to seven years to be “pre-moxed.” Id’ call that normal, after being served many such wines over the last 20 years. Presumably that’s not for lack of sulfur. I’ve always assume it’s too little acid. Cal chardonnays that go longer are the exception.

I am suggesting that some people undoubtedly make the mistake in question, as well as misunderstanding the “faux oxidation” you describe. Indeed, for those who stick mainly or exclusively to New World wines, a lot of the funky stuff that goes on in Old World wines can be foreign in all senses of the word.

Bob Parker said to tell you that you are wrong about the aging potential of Cali Chardonnays…

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: What interests me is that a very experienced person like Jasper Morris could have mistaken something in the wines for oxidation that wasn’t. I don’t really care about incorrect assessments by less informed people.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
http://www.newbordeaux.com/#!dubourdieu-premox-in-whites/c1lot

Here is that article somebody posted three months ago.
It is well worth reading. Lalou always says, The lees nourish the wine. Here is scientific proof.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Todd, I sold French-made fillers and corkers in the '80s.At that time vacuum corkers were rare in France.
Most wineries stood their bottles up for a few days to let the corks seat. Those wines aged just fine.

A vacuum corker should pull oxygen out of the bottle instead of compressing it. I don’t know how many French wineries now have vacuum corkers. Certainly one sees some very modern bottling lines at the bigger companies. These machines are designed to keep O2 pickup to a minimum.

There does seem to be a response to the premox issue of
1/everyone else has it too
2/the problem is being exaggerated and if you people actually knew how to taste…

A friend of mine gave a talk in Beaune about oxidation in wine two years ago. He told me he got 2 hours of questions.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked: John is right in that most California chardonnay is designed to be drunk right away and nobody should be surprised if the wine is dead seven years on, just as nobody saves their Macon Cave de Vire for seven years. My point has been that the wines that do age well (ABC, Mt Eden, others) are still aging well.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: I picked up a couple of 20-year-old chardonnays at Chambers Street a year or two ago – a Chalone and a Ch. St. Jean that were in the remainders bin. To my surprise, they were glorious. But those are certainly the exception. Can’t say I’ve ever had an old ABC, but the couple of old Mt. Edens were kind of a revelation.

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked:
Mel Knox:

Todd, I sold French-made fillers and corkers in the '80s.At that time vacuum corkers were rare in France.
Most wineries stood their bottles up for a few days to let the corks seat. Those wines aged just fine.

A vacuum corker should pull oxygen out of the bottle instead of compressing it. I don’t know how many French wineries now have vacuum corkers. Certainly one sees some very modern bottling lines at the bigger companies. These machines are designed to keep O2 pickup to a minimum.

There does seem to be a response to the premox issue of
1/everyone else has it too
2/the problem is being exaggerated and if you people actually knew how to taste…

A friend of mine gave a talk in Beaune about oxidation in wine two years ago. He told me he got 2 hours of questions.

We may be in agrrement Mel. My point is that having measured the vacumm for years now, and starting in 1996, too much of a vacuum is not helpful at all. No vacuum is much preferred IMO. One thing I don’t hear people discuss is the coincidence of vacuum utilization and the rise of premox… Too much vacuum will pull air into the wine and throw your free SO2 levels out the window.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Todd,
I have an idea: why don t you write all the domaines with premox and ask them if their vacuum corker is the problem. I am sure you will get lots of answers.

Your thesis about vacuum corkers is interesting. I heard winemakers say (late 90s) that because they were biodynamic etc they were lowering SO2 levels. I also think that wineries sophisticated enough to use vacuum corkers measure dissolved O2 in the wine. But who knows?

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked: Sharpening my quill now!

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

What interests me is that a very experienced person like Jasper Morris could have mistaken something in the wines for oxidation that wasn’t. I don’t really care about incorrect assessments by less informed people.

Whether I’m “informed” or not, I am 'experienced". I have mistaken this several times in Dauvissat Chablis over the years. Though some were clearly oxidized, some improved. When I tried to raise this with Vincent Dauvissat, very early on…around 2000…the discussion was not well received…even though some of them had recovered nicely with long aeration. So, I still don’t have any sense of this from the “horse’s mouth”. But, I’ve experienced it with his wines and with a few other wines.

I wonder what those who quickly throw the wines into the drain are concluding.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
I opened three bottles of 1999 Dauvissat ‘Forets’ recently Stuart. One was splendid, two were badly oxidised. No amount of oxygen could bring back the shagged ones. All came from the same case. The only variable between these three bottles is perhaps a microsecond during bottling or the seal. I blame the cork seal.

Over the past fortnight I have had a 2008 Grosset Polish Hill Riesling, 2011 Pikes Merle Riesling, 2008 Yattarna Chardonnay and 2006 Shaw and Smith Chardonnay. All aging perfectly under screwcap, not a hint of oxidation and obviously no tca.

Best Regards
Jeremy

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
My first encounter with premox was at IPNC around 2002. Two amazing collectors I know brought --among other things–about 8 Montrachets from the 1995 vintage. Over half were kaput. This was judged not by some collection of newbies but by people with an average tasting lifespan of Burgundy around 35 years, inc some winemakers.

Tell these guys they were just imagining it.

Fortunately they also brought some Sauternes and Vintage Port.

I do think the situation is getting better.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

I picked up a couple of 20-year-old chardonnays at Chambers Street a year or two ago – a Chalone and a Ch. St. Jean that were in the remainders bin. To my surprise, they were glorious. But those are certainly the exception. Can’t say I’ve ever had an old ABC, but the couple of old Mt. Edens were kind of a revelation.

The older Chalone whites were outstanding.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This is an endlessly interesting subject.

One question raised by Jasper’s article is whether ostensibly premoxed wines that ‘recover’ and freshen up with decanting, even improving in color, ever show as well as indisputably correct bottles. I have had mid-1990s Lafon bottles that have certainly improved and brightened up with decanting, but never hit the heights of the great vintages of the 1970s and 1980s that I have encountered.

Another question is whether any one else with deep experience agrees with Jasper’s contention that the 1996 white Burgundies have recovered from premox. In fact, I was expecting this to excite more controversy. The tiny sample of bottles I’ve had lately—with their sherry-and-chicken broth aromatics, their deeply oxidized colors and their weirdly bleached corks—show no signs of ever being fit for human consumption. Of course, perhaps the argument is that some 1996s (like the latter) really are oxidized, whereas others were just going through a ‘phase’… But I would be surprised if wines like the 1996 Lafon Meursaults or the 1996 Etienne Sauzet Pulignys ever come back from the place to which they appear to have gone.

The Dubourdieu and Lavigne article also begs a number of questions, most obviously concerning two of their recommendations, 1) that the must be protected by SO2 and inert gas, and 2) that alcoholic and malolactic fermentation proceed rapidly. Neither of these practices were consistently part of pre-premox Burgundy winemaking as I understand it, where long, slow fermentation and malo were often the norm (at least in cold cellars, so perhaps not in Puligny), and where no inert gas was available to ‘protect’ the must. And yet, many of the wines made in that era are cruising along beautifully and look and taste more youthful than wines made as many as two decades later.

But multi-factorial problems are always going to be hard to solve.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: William, I’m completely in the same boat as you. I opened a 99 coche dury meursault last week. It was pretty premoxy that night. The next day it was less premoxy, fruit came out and it brightened up a bit but it never completely shed the nutty and lingering apple flavors.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Great thread! Three things to add.

Lower pH aids SO2 efficacy. So Cali Chards lacking acid(higher pH) simply don’t reap the protective benefit of the SO2 additions. Especially over time.

The assertion that early protection of the must with SO2 and/or inert gas is beneficial for premox is questionable. I have been delaying SO2 additions in white wines for a number of years and doing numerous experiments without SO2(at processing stage only). I also see no benefit in rushing malo. This is a reductive process and one that refreshes the wine and delays the true “oxidative” start to a wines life/evolution. Even if only a few months, that is still beneficial.

A number of years ago I read an article on the oxidative nature of Grenache Rosé and how Rhone producers were macro-oxidizing the wines, then fermenting them-which brings them back to fresh. With the result being more stable/less easily oxidized pink wines. That study led me to experiment with oxidative periods ahead of the fermentations, both primary and malolactic. Even with extended barrel aging regimens, I feel my white wines are less prone to oxidation than ever. I also delay pressing white fruit for 24-48 hours after picking so some of the juice is very oxidized as it comes from the press.

Last-I opened a set of 2006 Matello Pinot Noirs in 375ml in 2014 for my harvest crew. I was extremely disappointed to see them all oxidized. I am quite familiar with Jura wines in the oxidative style, and enjoy sherry regularly. These bottles simply appeared to be very over the hill/dead. I was extremely disappointed and left them by the lab sink to dump once my fragile ego had recovered. 30 minutes later I came back in to do the deed(if this sounds melodramatic please note that I made 500 cases of wine in 2006, and have maybe 4 cases of the 2006 vintage left) and decided to do one last check to see what I could learn. All were fresh, showing quite well, and no real signs of oxidation could be detected. Were they 100%, honestly I don’t know. This appears in approximately 75% of my 2006 wines. Of the remaining 25%, half are fresh and as hoped, the remaining half are dead. Of the 75%, the oxidized quality does seem to inhibit some of them, with a freshening happening but not a complete recovery.

I am not an MW like Jasper but I have 14 harvests and an equal amount of years in restaurant as a sommelier/wine professional to match. I know oxidized and dead wines, this was the first time I have ever seen this. My only guess/theory: 2006 is a very warm Oregon vintage with moderate pH. More important, harvest conditions were hot. Even with our best attempts at controlling picks and getting harvested early was a significant challenge. In each pick, except one, I had fruit temperatures that ranged from 58-90 degrees F. The exception delivered late and all the fruit was 88-90 F, and fermenting as it crossed the sorting line. It’s the fastest ferment starts I have ever had, and the highest SO2 adds I have used. Anecdotal at best, but we pick early and quick to get cool fruit and look for longer pre-ferment periods.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Marcus – Thanks for a fascinating post.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
WineBerserkers – 17 Nov 15

A great day at Au Bon Climat with the Mind Behind

A good friend called to see if I could arrange a winery/ tasting visit for him and 4 others with Jim Clendenen at Au Bon Climat. I was happy to do that knowing and appreciating both parties well. Tastings at the winery are not usually done. There...

John,
Here are some tasting notes on older ABC whites.

Marcus,
How come wineries like ABC, Chalone and Mt Eden have made age-worthy Chardonnay for decades?? Are they just adding more SO2?? Is it the lees contact?? Chalone harvests (harvested?? what will happen here??) grapes in August and September.

I do think some of you are concentrating on the latter part of the Lavigne/Dubourdieu article and missing its main point: that compounds in the lees fight against oxidation. Glutathione is singled out as important. Many winemakers have problems with their glutes because of the gluten-free diet. Lees are rich in glutathione.

I disagree with some of the points of the article myself. Many domaines use high percentages of new oak and their wines age just fine.

In the 70s and 80s people who did not like a red wine often said they were ‘vinegary’. Then there was a rash of people finding TCA where none existed. Maybe now it is true that unsophisticated tasters are finding premox where it does not exist. And how long should a white Burgundy age anyway? 5 years? 10? 20??

link_counts.title: A great day at Au Bon Climat with the Mind Behind - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Hi Mel, very intriguing questions and thoughts.

Regarding ABC, Mt.Eden(my faves for Cali Chard), and Chalone. In my experience, all of these producers seem to have among the brighter acidity levels, along with Mayacamas whose wines also age very well. I don’t think they are having to add more SO2 because they have good pH in the wines for aging from the beginning.

In Oregon, many of us focused on white varietals including Chardonnay(myself, Crowley, Walter Scott, and Teutonic all come to mind)are picking earlier than many used to. I suspect that early picking plays a part in why Chalone wines still have great balance. Even if that pick call is in August(huge speculation on my part with zero actual knowledge).

I completely agree that lees contact aids durability later on. I rack our whites from the lees just before bottling. I also only settle heavy solids from the juice before fermentation, racking to barrel while the juice is still slightly cloudy. This increases phenolics that take time to resolve but (IMHO) impart components that are necessary anti-oxidants. (Our Chardonnays are typically in barrel for 20 months, Pinot Gris 6-7 or 13-15 months, and Alsatien varietals 6-8 months).

I also feel new wood is helpful for durability, oak tannins are absorbed by the wines and are another layer of protection. This is helpful for Chardonnay in particular where the malolactic fermentation bumps pH higher than the no malo whites like Riesling and Pinot Blanc. I believe that the new oak used for our Chardonnays helps balance that. However, the longest lived white I make is a field blend of Riesling, Pinot Blanc, Pinot Noir, and Gewürztraminer done in VERY neutral barrique and Riesling, also done in neutral barrique.

Additionally-I use 860L and 500L barrels for much of my Chardonnay and some Pinot Gris, the bigger volumes of wine are a more reductive environment.
Also by not racking(or stirring) my wines often have a bit more retained CO2 in the wine, which IMHO, also keeps the wines fresh.
Last-traditional caves in Burgundy are quite cool for fermentation, with the ferments themselves providing much of the heat. This results in long, slow, (worrisome to the winemaker) ferments. As winemaking modernizes, the temptation to reduce our own worry as well as move the wine to a safer place microbially(under SO2) creeps in, we can forget that the long slow path is also possibly where integral aspects of the wine are formed.

To explore that idea, my primary ferments are quite cold, and typically last from 3-12 months. This is a reductive process and also a period where the lees are in constant circulation vs. a ferment finished by Thanksgiving. This year about 18% of my ferments have finished as of Dec. 1st, and we started about 25 days earlier than normal. This is only my belief, but I feel this is a huge part of making complex and durable wines. It is a huge number of extra hours playing mother hen to the wines. I check Brix twice a week on the whites, and go through daily to look for bubbles in ferm-locks and smell for issues. Doing that over 6 months(an average) instead of 6 weeks adds up. It gives an idea of how important a part of the process I think this is.

Complex questions and my thoughts above are only that. Some may turn out to have long term merit and some not. Only the wines will tell.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
Marcus,
Chalone has always been an enigma to me in that temperature wise it’s better suited to Syrah and Cabernet
but the Chardonnay doesn’t know that. The wines that aged well were made under a regime that was very Burgundian in its winemaking. How the current wines will age I do not know.
Basically Dick Graff went to Burgundy and came back and did everything the way they did it at DRC.

Mt Eden is in some ways also the spawn of Dick G.

ABC does pick from cooler locations. I can assure you lees play an important part in winemaking there.

You may not know that Eric Hamacher and i made Oregon Chardonnay together for five years, beginning in 1995. I have felt for a long time that great Chardonnays could be made there, among other places.

In the '70s many California winemakers fermented chardonnay at low temps…the wine would go dry in February…sometimes these wines had a tutti fruity bubble gum quality so beware!
